questions.explanation: cloudbuild.yaml supports a top-level artifacts section where you declare objects with a location (gs:// bucket path) and paths (glob). Cloud Build uploads matching files after the build completes, and they appear in the build's artifact manifest. Using gsutil cp in a step works but bypasses the native artifact tracking that makes files linkable from the build record. logsBucket controls only build logs, not artifacts.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your organization mandates that all Cloud Build steps use only approved base images stored internally, and no step may pull from Docker Hub. You also need to set a global environment variable ENVIRONMENT=production visible to every step without repeating it. Which pair of cloudbuild.yaml features satisfies both requirements?
options: Set the name field of each step to your Artifact Registry path, and use the top-level options.env list to declare global environment variables
Use the images field at the top level to whitelist Docker Hub images, and set environment variables in each step individually
Set the logsBucket to block external image pulls, and use substitutions instead of environment variables
Configure a build trigger filter that rejects steps with Docker Hub image references, and use a secret manager secret for the environment value
questions.explanation: In cloudbuild.yaml, the name field of each step specifies the container image to run; pointing it to your Artifact Registry ensures no Docker Hub images are pulled. The top-level options.env list defines environment variables that are injected into every step, avoiding repetition. The images field is for publishing final images, not whitelisting sources. Substitutions are for parameterizing values, not for injecting runtime environment variables globally.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your team is migrating container images from Container Registry to Artifact Registry. Which statement correctly describes a key operational difference between the two services?
options: Artifact Registry supports only Docker images; Container Registry supports Helm charts and npm packages
Artifact Registry supports multiple format repositories (Docker, Maven, npm, Python, etc.) within a single project and region, while Container Registry supports only Docker images
Container Registry is the newer service and is preferred for all new workloads on Google Cloud
Artifact Registry and Container Registry share the same storage bucket; they differ only in the API endpoint
questions.explanation: Artifact Registry is the successor to Container Registry and supports multiple artifact formats including Docker images, Maven, npm, Python, Go, and Helm charts in regionally-scoped repositories. Container Registry supports only Docker images and is in maintenance mode. They use separate storage backends; Container Registry stores images in Cloud Storage buckets while Artifact Registry uses its own managed storage.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: A security auditor requires that every container image stored in Artifact Registry be automatically scanned for known OS and language-package vulnerabilities, with no manual step. How do you enable this?
options: Enable the Container Analysis API and turn on automatic scanning in the Artifact Registry repository settings; scans run on every push
Schedule a daily Cloud Build job that runs trivy against each image in the repository
Configure a Binary Authorization policy that blocks deployment but does not scan images
Enable VPC Service Controls on the project; this implicitly activates vulnerability scanning
questions.explanation: Artifact Registry integrates with Container Analysis to provide on-push vulnerability scanning. Once the Container Analysis API is enabled and scanning is turned on for the repository, every newly pushed image is scanned automatically and findings appear as Occurrences in the Container Analysis API. Binary Authorization enforces attestations at deploy time but does not scan images. VPC Service Controls restrict API access perimeters and have no effect on vulnerability scanning.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: You are setting up access control for an Artifact Registry Docker repository. A Cloud Build service account in project A must be able to push images, and a GKE node service account in project B must be able to pull images. Which TWO IAM bindings are required? (Choose TWO)
options: Grant the Cloud Build service account from project A the roles/artifactregistry.writer role on the Artifact Registry repository in project A
Grant the GKE node service account from project B the roles/artifactregistry.reader role on the Artifact Registry repository in project A
Grant the Cloud Build service account from project A the roles/storage.objectCreator role on the GCS bucket backing Artifact Registry
Grant the GKE node service account from project B the roles/container.developer role in project B
questions.explanation: roles/artifactregistry.writer grants push (write) access to a repository and is the correct role for the Cloud Build service account that publishes images. roles/artifactregistry.reader grants pull (read) access and is the correct role for the GKE node service account in the consuming project. Artifact Registry does not use GCS buckets, so storage roles are irrelevant. roles/container.developer is a GKE cluster administration role unrelated to image registry access.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: A financial services company requires that all container images deployed to production carry a cryptographic attestation confirming they passed a security scan and were built by an approved Cloud Build pipeline. After pushing a signed image to Artifact Registry, what GCP mechanism enforces that only attested images are deployed to GKE?
options: Artifact Registry tag immutability, which prevents image tags from being overwritten
Binary Authorization, which evaluates attestations at pod admission time and blocks any image that lacks a valid attestation from a trusted attestor
Cloud Armor policies applied to the GKE ingress, which inspect image metadata before allowing traffic
Container Analysis Occurrences, which automatically block deployment if a CRITICAL vulnerability is found
questions.explanation: Binary Authorization is Google Cloud's deploy-time admission controller for GKE. It evaluates attestations stored in Container Analysis against a policy and rejects pods that reference images without valid attestations signed by a configured trusted attestor. Tag immutability prevents tag mutation but does not enforce attestations. Cloud Armor operates at the network/HTTP layer and has no visibility into image metadata. Container Analysis records findings but does not directly block Kubernetes deployments.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your organization runs Cloud Build pipelines that must reach internal services hosted on a VPC, including a private Artifact Registry mirror and an on-premises test database connected via Cloud VPN. The default public Cloud Build pool cannot reach these resources. What is the correct solution?
options: Add firewall rules to allow Cloud Build's public IP ranges into the VPC
Create a Cloud Build private pool peered to the VPC that hosts the private resources; build jobs running on the private pool can reach VPC-internal addresses
Enable Private Google Access on the VPC subnet; this allows Cloud Build to connect to VPC resources
Deploy a proxy VM in the VPC and route all build traffic through it using cloudbuild.yaml step environment variables
questions.explanation: Cloud Build private pools are dedicated worker pools that can be VPC-peered to your network, giving build steps direct access to RFC 1918 addresses inside the VPC including private Artifact Registry mirrors, Cloud SQL instances, and on-premises resources reached via VPN or Interconnect. The default pool shares public infrastructure and cannot reach private VPC resources. Private Google Access enables VMs without external IPs to reach Google APIs, not the reverse situation. A proxy VM introduces operational overhead and is not the recommended pattern.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your security team requires that Cloud Build workers for regulated workloads never have internet egress -- all traffic must stay within Google's network or route to approved internal endpoints. You have provisioned a private pool. Which configuration enforces no-internet-egress on that pool?
options: Set the privatePoolV1Config.networkConfig.egressOption to NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS when creating or updating the private pool
Apply a VPC firewall rule with deny-all egress on the VPC; private pool workers automatically inherit it
Enable VPC Service Controls on the project; this blocks all internet egress from compute resources including Cloud Build
Set the cloudbuild.yaml top-level option timeout to a low value to limit outbound connection attempts
questions.explanation: Cloud Build private pools expose a networkConfig.egressOption field. Setting it to NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS configures the worker pool so that all traffic is routed through the peered VPC and no direct internet egress is permitted. VPC firewall deny-all egress rules apply to VM instances in the VPC but private pool workers reside in a Google-managed tenant project and are not directly governed by customer firewall rules unless traffic routes through the peered network. VPC Service Controls restricts API access at the project boundary but does not block internet egress from compute workers.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: A team provisions a Cloud Build private pool to build container images that are pushed to a private Artifact Registry repository in the same VPC. After setup, builds fail because the workers cannot resolve the Artifact Registry hostname. What is the most likely cause and fix?
options: The private pool's machine type is too small to run DNS resolution; upgrade to a larger machine type

Private Google Access is not enabled on the VPC subnet used for private pool peering, so the workers cannot resolve and reach Google API endpoints over internal addresses; enable Private Google Access on that subnet
The Artifact Registry repository is in the wrong region; move it to match the private pool region
Cloud Build private pools do not support Artifact Registry; use Container Registry instead
questions.explanation: When a Cloud Build private pool is peered to a VPC, the workers use the customer VPC's DNS and routing. Private Google Access must be enabled on the subnet so that instances (and peered workers) can reach Google APIs such as Artifact Registry using internal IP addresses without traversing the internet. Without Private Google Access, DNS resolution for *.pkg.dev fails or routes incorrectly and push/pull operations time out. Machine type and region do not affect DNS resolution. Artifact Registry is fully supported with private pools.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service
